comprehensive_summary:
Fyodor Dostoevsky (Fyodor Mikhailovich Dostoevsky) was a Russian novelist and proto-existentialist author. He was imprisoned after a mock execution for membership in the Fourierist Petrashevsky Circle and spent four years in a Siberian prison camp, which he fictionalized in The House of the Dead.

The Brothers Karamazov is Dostoevsky's final and most philosophically dense novel. The title brothers are Dmitri, Ivan, Alyosha (Alexei), and the illegitimate Smerdyakov. Ivan tells the parable of the Grand Inquisitor to Alyosha, in which Christ returns to 16th-century Seville and is imprisoned by the Inquisitor, who argues the church follows the Devil's principles. Ivan declares "if God is dead, everything is permitted" and tells his brother "it's not God that I don't accept... only I most respectfully return him the ticket." In the chapter "Rebellion," Ivan describes a baby ripped apart by hunting dogs. Dmitri is tried for the murder of their father Fyodor Pavlovich, but the actual perpetrator is Smerdyakov. Evidence includes a letter on a tavern bill and 3,000 rubles from an envelope addressed to Grushenka. The father's Adam's apple and hooked nose provoke Dmitri as he stares through a garden window clutching a brass pestle. Alyosha's mentor is the elder Father Zosima. Grushenka tells the parable of the onion to Alyosha (a woman's one good deed of giving an onion). Alyosha dreams of Zosima in heaven during a passage about the Wedding at Cana. Father Paissy reads over Zosima's coffin. Freud's essay "Dostoevsky and Parricide" blamed a desire to commit patricide for Dostoevsky's epilepsy. Mikhail Bakhtin used Menippean satire to analyze the Grand Inquisitor in Problems of Dostoevsky's Poetics. The father compares an atheist to von Sohn and ponders "iron hooks" in hell.

Crime and Punishment follows Raskolnikov, who murders a pawnbroker and her sister with an axe. Foreshadowed by: a student in a bar denouncing the old woman as a parasite; Raskolnikov's dream of a mare being beaten to death by Mikolka. The inspector Porfiry questions him about his article "On Crime," which argues "extraordinary" people have the right to "transgress the law." Porfiry asks if he believes in the raising of Lazarus. Raskolnikov forces the prostitute Sonia to read him the Lazarus story. Sonia's father Marmeladov is killed by a carriage. Svidrigailov shoots himself with Dunya's revolver after telling a soldier he's headed for "America." Raskolnikov is exiled to Siberia; Sonia follows him. Dostoevsky's extensive reading of Dickens during his Siberian imprisonment inspired the character Nellie.

Notes from Underground opens "I am a sick man... I am a spiteful man." The unnamed narrator laments not being able to become an insect and describes the pleasure of a toothache. He ridicules Chernyshevsky's utopian Crystal Palace. In "Apropos of the Wet Snow," he behaves poorly at Zverkov's party, challenges Ferfichkin to a duel, and ridicules the prostitute Liza. He fantasizes about transferring Lake Como to Rome and hosting parties at the Villa Borghese after forcing the Pope to retire to Brazil. Bakhtin analyzed the "dialogized self-consciousness" of this novella. Rene Girard's study examines the irony of "I am alone and they are everyone."

The Idiot features the epileptic Prince Myshkin, who may have experienced Stendhal syndrome viewing Holbein's Dead Christ. During an anti-Catholic tirade at the Yepanchin estate, he gesticulates wildly and destroys a Chinese vase. Nastasya Filippovna throws 100,000 rubles into her fireplace to mock Ganya's avarice. Rogozhin murders Nastasya. Myshkin returns to a sanatorium in Switzerland. Aglaya is a love interest.

Demons (also translated as The Possessed or The Devils) is an allegory about nihilists led by Stavrogin, inspired by Sergey Nechayev. A censored chapter ("At Tikhon's") has Stavrogin confessing to a monk about raping an 11-year-old girl. Kirillov wants to kill himself as an act of self-determination. Pyotr Verkhovensky manipulates the radicals. Stavrogin hangs himself. Liza Tushin is killed by a mob during a fire. Virginia Woolf helped translate the censored chapter.

Other works include: Poor Folk (pauper Makar writes letters to his second-cousin), The Double (Golyadkin is thrown out of a party and carted to an asylum), White Nights (a lonely narrator develops feelings for Nastenka in St. Petersburg), "The Dream of a Ridiculous Man" (pulled from his grave and shown an Earth-like planet), "A Gentle Creature" (a husband drives his 16-year-old wife to suicide), "An Honest Thief" (riding breeches stolen by the drunken Yemelyan), "Bobok" (dead people in a cemetery discuss philosophy), "The Beggar Boy at Christ's Christmas Tree," and the unfinished Netochka Nezvanova.

Dostoevsky suffered from epilepsy (Geschwind syndrome is sometimes named for him). Nabokov disliked him, parodying him as Hermann Karlovich in Despair. Walter Kaufmann's anthology Existentialism from Dostoevsky to Sartre uses him as the starting point. Camus called him an "absurd creator" in The Myth of Sisyphus. Simone de Beauvoir aimed to disprove his statement about God. Bakhtin introduced "polyphony" and "unfinalizability" through his study of Dostoevsky. J.M. Coetzee's The Master of Petersburg fictionalizes him. Leonid Tsypkin's Summer in Baden-Baden recounts his wife Anna copying a novel about his roulette addiction. He called the concept "the man in man" (what social sciences cannot explain). Ironic process theory (the white bear problem) comes from his travel notes. Raymond Carver wrote a screenplay about him. Ursula K. Le Guin said "forgetting" him inspired Omelas.

works.name: The Brothers Karamazov
works.description: Dostoevsky's final novel about the Karamazov brothers. Ivan tells the parable of the Grand Inquisitor. Dmitri is tried for patricide but Smerdyakov is the true killer. Alyosha is guided by Father Zosima.

clues.clue: Ivan tells the parable of the Grand Inquisitor to Alyosha; Christ returns to 16th-century Seville and is imprisoned
examples: This author envisioned a sixteenth-century jail cell containing the returned Jesus in the poema of the Grand Inquisitor.
A nihilist character argues that the church follows the Devil's principles in Christ's name.

clues.clue: Ivan declares "if God is dead, everything is permitted" / "I most respectfully return him the ticket"
examples: Simone de Beauvoir aimed to disprove this author's statement, 'if God is dead, everything is permitted.'
One character tells his brother, 'It's not God that I don't accept... only I most respectfully return him the ticket.'

clues.clue: Dmitri tried for patricide; actual killer is Smerdyakov; evidence includes 3,000 rubles, a letter, a brass pestle
examples: Evidence for this crime, including a letter scrawled on a tavern bill and the theft of 3,000 rubles.
The Adam's apple and hooked nose of his 'tormentor' prompt a man to consider this crime.

clues.clue: "Rebellion" chapter: Ivan describes a baby ripped apart by hunting dogs; Le Guin's Omelas inspired by a society dependent on a "little victim"
examples: A character describes a baby ripped apart by hunting dogs in a chapter titled 'Rebellion.'
Le Guin quipped that 'forgetting' this author inspired Omelas.

clues.clue: Grushenka tells the parable of the onion to Alyosha; inspired Akutagawa's "The Spider's Thread"
examples: Grushenka analogizes her one 'good deed' to an onion.
Ryunosuke Akutagawa's 'The Spider's Thread' was inspired by a parable about this food.

clues.clue: Alyosha's mentor is Father Zosima; Alyosha dreams of him during the Wedding at Cana
examples: Several scenes set in the hermitage of Elder Zosima.
While listening to a passage about the Wedding at Cana, Alyosha has a dream of Father Zosima.

clues.clue: Freud's "Dostoevsky and Parricide" blamed a desire to commit patricide for his epilepsy
examples: Freud blamed a desire to commit this crime for Dostoevsky's epilepsy.
Joseph Frank refuted a 1928 essay partly titled for this crime.

works.name: Crime and Punishment
works.description: Raskolnikov murders a pawnbroker and her sister with an axe. Foreshadowed by dreams and overheard conversations. Inspector Porfiry investigates. Sonia follows Raskolnikov to Siberian exile.

clues.clue: Raskolnikov murders a pawnbroker (and her sister) with an axe; exiled to Siberia; Sonia follows him
examples: Raskolnikov murders a pawnbroker in Crime and Punishment.
This Dostoevsky novel ends with Sonya following Raskolnikov to Siberia.

clues.clue: Porfiry questions Raskolnikov about his article "On Crime": "extraordinary" people can "transgress the law"
examples: The inspector Porfiry quizzes the protagonist on an article suggesting that some men have a right to commit murder.
Porfiry Petrovich questions the protagonist because it suggests that 'extraordinary' people have the right to 'transgress the law.'

clues.clue: Raskolnikov forces Sonia to read the story of Lazarus; later reminded of it before confessing
examples: A crazed Raskolnikov commands Sonia to read him this story.
Crime and Punishment's final paragraphs refer to this story.

clues.clue: Foreshadowing dream: Mikolka beats a mare to death; a student denounces the pawnbroker as a parasite
examples: A flashback dream in which Raskolnikov witnesses a group led by Mikolka perform this violent action.
The main character overhears a student denouncing an old woman as a parasite.

clues.clue: Svidrigailov shoots himself with Dunya's revolver; tells a soldier he's headed for "America"
examples: Svidrigailov kills himself with Dunya's revolver.
Svidrigailov informs a soldier dressed in an Achilles helmet that he's headed for 'America.'

clues.clue: Marmeladov (Sonia's father) killed by a carriage; Raskolnikov gives money to the family
examples: Raskolnikov gives money to the family of the prostitute Sonya after he witnesses her drunkard father Marmeladov being killed.

works.name: Notes from Underground
works.description: Opens "I am a sick man... I am a spiteful man." The unnamed narrator ridicules Chernyshevsky's Crystal Palace, describes the pleasure of a toothache, and behaves poorly at Zverkov's party before ridiculing the prostitute Liza.

clues.clue: Opens "I am a sick man... I am a spiteful man"; narrator laments not being able to become an insect
examples: One of this author's narrators describes the pleasure of a toothache after opening, 'I am a sick man... I am a spiteful man.'
A novella beginning 'I am a sick man... I am a spiteful man.'

clues.clue: "Apropos of the Wet Snow": narrator behaves poorly at Zverkov's party; ridicules prostitute Liza
examples: That character created by this author ridicules the prostitute Liza.
He deliberately bumps into an officer on the street in the section 'Apropos of the Wet Snow.'

clues.clue: Ridicules Chernyshevsky's utopian Crystal Palace
examples: One of this author's characters ridicules Chernyshevsky's utopian symbol of the Crystal Palace.
This author critiqued another author's description of the utopian Crystal Palace.

clues.clue: Describes the pleasure of a toothache; fantasizes about moving Lake Como to Rome
examples: This character equates the laws of nature with impossibilities and declares that the moaning of a man with a toothache is evidence of pleasure.
This character has fantasies of transferring Lake Como to Rome.

works.name: The Idiot
works.description: The epileptic Prince Myshkin may have experienced Stendhal syndrome viewing Holbein's Dead Christ. Nastasya Filippovna throws 100,000 rubles into a fireplace. Rogozhin murders Nastasya. Myshkin returns to Switzerland.

clues.clue: Prince Myshkin may have experienced Stendhal syndrome viewing Holbein's Dead Christ; launches anti-Catholic tirade and destroys a Chinese vase
examples: This author may have experienced Stendhal syndrome when viewing Holbein's Dead Christ.
During an anti-Catholic tirade, another protagonist gesticulates wildly and destroys a Chinese vase.

clues.clue: Nastasya Filippovna throws 100,000 rubles into her fireplace to mock Ganya's avarice
examples: A woman perpetrates burning money at her own dinner party to mock the avarice of her suitor Ganya.

clues.clue: Rogozhin murders Nastasya; Myshkin returns to a sanatorium in Switzerland
examples: Rogozhin murders a woman he loves, Nastasya Filippovna.
Myshkin is sent back to a sanatorium in Switzerland.

works.name: Demons (The Possessed)
works.description: An allegory about nihilists led by Stavrogin, inspired by Nechayev. A censored chapter has Stavrogin confessing to a monk. Kirillov wants to kill himself as an act of self-determination. Stavrogin hangs himself.

clues.clue: Allegory about nihilists led by Stavrogin; inspired by Nechayev; Pyotr Verkhovensky manipulates radicals
examples: An allegory about a circle of nihilists led by Stavrogin.
Stepan Verkhovensky is dismayed to find his son Pyotr turning into a nihilistic would-be revolutionary.

clues.clue: Censored chapter "At Tikhon's": Stavrogin confesses to raping an 11-year-old girl; Virginia Woolf helped translate it
examples: Virginia Woolf helped translate a sexually explicit chapter that was cut.
Nikolai Stavrogin gives a monk a lengthy document in which he admits to raping an 11-year-old girl.

clues.clue: Kirillov wants to kill himself to prove conquest of fear; falsely accepts blame for Shatov's murder
examples: Kirillov wants to kill himself as an apocalyptic act of self-determination proving his conquest of fear.

clues.clue: Stavrogin hangs himself at the end; Liza Tushin killed by a mob during a fire
examples: After he learns of Liza's death, Nikolai Stavrogin hangs himself.
During a fire, Liza Tushin is killed by a mob.

works.name: Other Works
works.description: Poor Folk, The Double, White Nights, The House of the Dead, "The Dream of a Ridiculous Man," "Bobok," "A Gentle Creature," "An Honest Thief," and others.

clues.clue: The House of the Dead: fictionalized prison memoir with a Dantesque Christmas bath scene
examples: He based his novel The House of the Dead on his prison experience.
A famously-Dantesque scene in which steam turns red off the backs of men taking a Christmas bath.

clues.clue: "White Nights": lonely narrator develops feelings for Nastenka in St. Petersburg
examples: A lonely man who wanders the streets of St. Petersburg and develops an affection for Nastenka.

clues.clue: "Bobok": dead people in a cemetery discuss philosophy; Baron Kilnevich exposes Tarasevich's embezzlement
examples: Characters with this trait discuss the philosopher Platon Nikolaevich and one of them repeatedly says 'Bobok.'
Baron Kilnevich tells other characters to cast aside all shame.

clues.clue: Poor Folk: pauper Makar writes letters to his second-cousin before she marries a rich widower
examples: This author created the pauper Makar, who writes letters to his second-cousin.

clues.clue: The Double: Golyadkin is thrown out of Klara's party and carted to an asylum
examples: A novella about the titular counselor Golyadkin, who is thrown out of Klara's birthday party.

clues.clue: "The Dream of a Ridiculous Man": pulled from grave and shown an Earth-like planet
examples: A shadowy figure pulls the narrator up from his grave and hurtles through space with him.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Russian proto-existentialist author. Imprisoned after mock execution for the Petrashevsky Circle. Suffered from epilepsy (Geschwind syndrome). Bakhtin's Problems of Dostoevsky's Poetics introduced polyphony and unfinalizability.

clues.clue: Imprisoned after mock execution for membership in Fourierist Petrashevsky Circle; spent four years in Siberian prison camp
examples: This author was imprisoned for his membership in a Fourierist discussion group.
This author was subjected to a mock execution and arrested after reading a critic's letter.

clues.clue: Bakhtin's Problems of Dostoevsky's Poetics: polyphony of voices, unfinalizability, dialogized self-consciousness
examples: A book on the problems of this author's poetics is by Mikhail Bakhtin.
Bakhtin introduced 'polyphony' and 'unfinalizability' through his study of this author.

clues.clue: Suffered from epilepsy; Geschwind syndrome sometimes named for him; paradigmatic sufferer of TLE
examples: This author, the paradigmatic sufferer of Geschwind syndrome.
This hypergraphic author whom Nabokov disliked.

clues.clue: Nabokov disliked him; parodied as Hermann Karlovich in Despair
examples: Nabokov disliked him so much that he parodied him in Despair as Hermann Karlovich.

clues.clue: J.M. Coetzee's The Master of Petersburg fictionalizes him; Tsypkin's Summer in Baden-Baden about his wife
examples: J M Coetzee's The Master of Petersburg fictionalizes him.
Leonid Tsypkin's Summer in Baden-Baden recounts his wife Anna copying out a novel.

clues.clue: Walter Kaufmann's Existentialism from Dostoevsky to Sartre; Camus called him an "absurd creator"
examples: The first author in the title of a Walter Kaufmann anthology.
The Myth of Sisyphus' third chapter argues that this author was an absurd creator.

clues.clue: Ironic process theory / "white bear problem" comes from his travel notes
examples: Ironic process theory is often named for a problem drawn from this author's travel notes about trying not to think of a white bear.

cards.front: Novel: Ivan tells Alyosha the parable of the Grand Inquisitor, in which Christ returns to 16th-century Seville and is imprisoned by a church leader who argues the church follows the Devil's principles
Novel: Ivan declares "if there is no immortality, then everything is permitted" and tells his brother "I most respectfully return him the ticket"
Novel: Dmitri is tried for patricide though Smerdyakov is the real killer; evidence includes 3,000 rubles and a brass pestle; Grushenka tells the parable of the onion
Novel: Raskolnikov murders a pawnbroker with an axe; inspector Porfiry questions him about his article arguing "extraordinary" people can "transgress the law"
Novel: Raskolnikov forces the prostitute Sonia to read the story of Lazarus; she later follows him to Siberian exile
Novel: the murder is foreshadowed by a dream of Mikolka beating a mare to death and a student calling the pawnbroker a parasite
Novel: Svidrigailov shoots himself with Dunya's revolver after telling a soldier he's headed for "America"
Novella: opens "I am a sick man... I am a spiteful man"; the narrator ridicules Chernyshevsky's Crystal Palace and the prostitute Liza in "Apropos of the Wet Snow"
Novel: the epileptic Prince Myshkin views Holbein's Dead Christ; Nastasya Filippovna throws 100,000 rubles into a fireplace; Rogozhin murders Nastasya
Novel: an allegory about nihilists led by Stavrogin; the censored chapter "At Tikhon's" describes a confession of rape; Kirillov kills himself to prove conquest of fear
Novel: a fictionalized Siberian prison memoir featuring a Dantesque scene where steam turns red off men taking a Christmas bath
cards.back: The House of the Dead (Fyodor Dostoevsky)

cards.front: Short Story: a lonely narrator wanders St. Petersburg and develops feelings for Nastenka, who is dismayed when her actual lover returns
Author: imprisoned after a mock execution for the Fourierist Petrashevsky Circle; spent four years in a Siberian prison camp
Author: Bakhtin's Problems of Dostoevsky's Poetics introduced "polyphony" and "unfinalizability" through studying this author's novels
Author: suffered from epilepsy (Geschwind syndrome); the "white bear problem" comes from this author's travel notes
Author: Nabokov parodied this author as Hermann Karlovich in Despair; J.M. Coetzee fictionalized him in The Master of Petersburg

summary: Fyodor Dostoevsky (Fyodor Mikhailovich Dostoevsky) was a Russian novelist and proto-existentialist author.
